title: Chicken tagine with figs
subtitle: Moroccan braised chicken with dried figs, honey, and almonds
excerpt: Chicken thighs braised with saffron, ginger, and cinnamon until tender, then dried figs added with honey to finish. The figs soften in the sauce and absorb the spiced cooking liquid; the honey rounds the whole thing. Scattered with toasted almonds and served with couscous.
sourceNotes: The sweet chicken tagine with dried fruit is one of the Moroccan festive dishes. Figs are used in season when fresh (late summer) and dried throughout the year. The combination of chicken, saffron, and dried figs is associated with the cooking of Fez.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the braised chicken and sauce without the almonds.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Can be made a day ahead. Reheat gently and add toasted almonds just before serving.

content.text: Bone-in, skin-on thighs are correct here — the bones enrich the sauce and the fat from the skin bastes the meat as it braises. The skin will soften (not stay crisp) in the braising liquid, but it adds body. If only skinless thighs are available, they work but the sauce will be lighter.

Dried figs vary considerably in sweetness and moisture. Soft, moist dried figs (Turkish or Greek) dissolve partially into the sauce, thickening it. Dry, hard dried figs hold their shape better but take longer to soften. Either is acceptable — the result is different but both are good.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a wide, heavy casserole over high heat. Season the {{chicken-thigh}} pieces with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper}}. Brown skin-side down for 5 minutes without moving, then turn and colour the other side for 2 minutes. Remove and set aside. Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 8 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, and {{cinnamon-ground}} and cook for 1 minute. Return the chicken to the pot. Pour in the saffron water and 300 ml of cold water. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook over low heat for 35 minutes.

Add the {{dried-figs}} and {{honey}} to the pot. Stir gently, cover, and cook for a further 15 minutes until the figs are soft and the chicken is cooked through. Toast the {{blanched-almonds}} in a dry pan until golden. Remove the lid from the tagine for the final 5 minutes if the sauce needs thickening. Taste and adjust seasoning. Serve with couscous, scattered with the toasted almonds.

items.cause: Figs very sweet or too much honey
items.fix: Add a squeeze of lemon juice at the end to balance. Start with 1 tablespoon of honey rather than 2 if your figs are already very sweet

items.symptom: Chicken falling off the bone before figs are added
items.cause: Heat too high during the initial braise
items.fix: The braise should be at a gentle simmer, not a boil. Reduce heat so only occasional bubbles break the surface

items.cause: Too much water added or lid not fitting well
items.fix: Remove the lid for the last 10 to 15 minutes and increase heat slightly to reduce

content.text: Sweet chicken tagines are associated with the refined urban cooking of Fez and the imperial cities, where the medieval tradition of combining poultry with dried fruit has been sustained. This dish appears at family celebrations and on the menu for guests. In Morocco the fresh fig version is made in August and September when the fruit is in season; the dried version is a year-round preparation. It represents the distinctly Moroccan approach to sweetness in savoury cooking, where honey and dried fruit are used with restraint as seasoning rather than as dessert.
